AI.tldr: A nonlinear grid-forming controller with voltage-formation guarantees, disturbance suppression, and CBF-based over-current protection that doesn't require grid voltage bounds or network parameters.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust grid-forming controller that ensures voltage formation despite unknown grid voltage disturbances, provides provable stability guarantees, and incorporates hard over-current protection without requiring knowledge of grid voltage bounds or network parameters beyond the PCC.
AI.method: Combines droop-based inner-outer architecture with backstepping voltage control, deadzone-adapted disturbance suppression (DADS) for robustness, and a CBF-based safety filter for over-current protection via quadratic programming. The controller treats grid voltage as unknown bounded disturbance and requires no network parameters beyond PCC.
AI.result: The controller achieves practical voltage regulation with asymptotic convergence to assignably small residual set, global well-posedness, bounded states, exponential error decay. CBF filter ensures strict current limits. Numerical results show improved transient performance and faster recovery during current-limiting events compared to conventional PI-based GFM control.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DADS-BS controller with CBF safety filter provides a robust, provably stable grid-forming solution with voltage formation guarantees and hard over-current protection, outperforming conventional approaches while requiring minimal system knowledge.

title: Safe and Robust Domains of Attraction for Discrete-Time Systems: A Set-Based Characterization and Certifiable Neural Network Estimation
summary: Analyzing nonlinear systems with attracting robust invariant sets (RISs) requires estimating their domains of attraction (DOAs). Despite extensive research, accurately characterizing DOAs for general nonlinear systems remains challenging due to both theoretical and computational limitations, particularly in the presence of uncertainties and state constraints. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for the accurate estimation of safe (state-constrained) and robust DOAs for discrete-time nonlinear uncertain systems with continuous dynamics, open safe sets, compact disturbance sets, and uniformly locally $\ell_p$-stable compact RISs. The notion of uniform $\ell_p$ stability is quite general and encompasses, as special cases, uniform exponential and polynomial stability. The DOAs are characterized via newly introduced value functions defined on metric spaces of compact sets. We establish their fundamental mathematical properties and derive the associated Bellman-type (Zubov-type) functional equations. Building on this characterization, we develop a physics-informed neural network (NN) framework to learn the corresponding value functions by embedding the derived Bellman-type equations directly into the training process. To obtain certifiable estimates of the safe robust DOAs from the learned neural approximations, we further introduce a verification procedure that leverages existing formal verification tools. The effectiveness and applicability of the proposed methodology are demonstrated through four numerical examples involving nonlinear uncertain systems subject to state constraints, and its performance is compared with existing methods from the literature.

AI.tldr: A framework for estimating safe robust domains of attraction for discrete-time nonlinear uncertain systems using value functions on metric spaces and physics-informed neural networks with formal verification.
AI.motivation: Accurately characterizing domains of attraction for general nonlinear systems with uncertainties and state constraints remains challenging due to theoretical and computational limitations, particularly for systems with attracting robust invariant sets.
AI.method: Introduces value functions defined on metric spaces of compact sets to characterize domains of attraction, establishes their mathematical properties and Bellman-type functional equations, then develops a physics-informed neural network framework that embeds these equations into training, followed by a formal verification procedure for certifiable estimates.
AI.result: The proposed methodology effectively estimates safe robust domains of attraction for nonlinear uncertain systems with state constraints, as demonstrated through four numerical examples, and shows improved performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a novel approach for accurate estimation of safe robust domains of attraction for discrete-time nonlinear uncertain systems, combining theoretical characterization with practical computational methods through physics-informed neural networks and formal verification.

title: Deep Q-Learning-Based Gain Scheduling for Nonlinear Quadcopter Dynamics
summary: This paper presents a deep Q-network (DQN)-based gain-scheduling framework for safety-critical quadcopter trajectory tracking. Instead of directly learning control inputs, the proposed approach selects from a finite set of pre-certified stabilizing gain vectors, enabling reinforcement learning to operate within a structured and stability-preserving control architecture. By exploiting the isotropic structure of the translational dynamics, feedback gains are shared across spatial axes to reduce dimensionality while preserving performance. The learned policy adapts feedback aggressiveness in real time, applying high authority during large transients and reducing gains near convergence to limit control effort. Simulation results using a high-fidelity nonlinear quadcopter model demonstrate accurate trajectory tracking, bounded attitude excursions, smooth transition to hover after the final time, and consistent reward improvement, validating the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed learning-based gain scheduling strategy.

AI.tldr: DQN-based gain-scheduling framework for quadcopter trajectory tracking that selects from pre-certified stabilizing gains rather than learning control inputs directly, ensuring stability while adapting feedback aggressiveness in real time.
AI.motivation: To develop a reinforcement learning approach for quadcopter control that operates within stability-preserving constraints, avoiding the safety risks of directly learning control inputs while enabling adaptive gain scheduling for improved performance.
AI.method: Deep Q-network (DQN) framework that selects from a finite set of pre-certified stabilizing gain vectors, exploiting isotropic translational dynamics to share gains across spatial axes, reducing dimensionality while maintaining performance.
AI.result: Simulation results using high-fidelity nonlinear quadcopter model demonstrate accurate trajectory tracking, bounded attitude excursions, smooth transition to hover, and consistent reward improvement, validating effectiveness and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed learning-based gain scheduling strategy successfully combines reinforcement learning with stability guarantees, enabling real-time adaptation of feedback aggressiveness while maintaining safety-critical performance in quadcopter control.

title: Can a Learner Regret Using a No-Regret Algorithm? A Control-Theoretic Study of Performance Dominance
summary: No-regret learning dynamics ensure that a learner asymptotically achieves an average reward no worse than that of any fixed strategy. This no-regret guarantee does not determine the value of the asymptotic average reward. Indeed, it is possible for different no-regret learning dynamics to exhibit different asymptotic average rewards when facing the same environment while both assure the no-regret guarantee. This paper asks whether a "free-lunch" phenomenon can arise among no-regret algorithms. Namely, is it possible for one no-regret learning rule to uniformly outperform another no-regret learning rule across all payoff environments. Stated differently, can a learner regret not using a particular no-regret algorithm? We consider generalized replicator dynamics (RD) as a cascade interconnection between a linear time-invariant (LTI) system and the softmax nonlinearity. Varying this LTI system leads to different realizations of replicator dynamics, including so-called anticipatory RD, exponential RD, and other forms of higher-order RD. Setting the LTI system to be an integrator realizes standard RD, which is known to satisfy the no-regret property. Within this framework, we analyze and compare various realizations of these generalized realizations RD by varying the LTI system. We first formulate performance comparison as a passivity property of an associated comparison system and establish "local" dominance results, i.e., comparing the asymptotic performance near an equilibrium payoff vector. We then cast performance comparison between a form of anticipatory RD and standard RD as an optimal-control problem. We show that the minimal achievable cumulative reward gap is zero, thereby establishing global dominance of anticipatory RD across all payoff environments and establishing a "free lunch" among no-regret learning dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that anticipatory replicator dynamics globally dominate standard replicator dynamics across all payoff environments, creating a "free lunch" among no-regret learning algorithms.
AI.motivation: Different no-regret learning dynamics can achieve different asymptotic average rewards while both satisfying the no-regret guarantee. The paper investigates whether one no-regret algorithm can uniformly outperform another across all environments (a "free-lunch" phenomenon).
AI.method: Models generalized replicator dynamics as a cascade interconnection between a linear time-invariant (LTI) system and softmax nonlinearity. Analyzes performance comparison using passivity properties and formulates the comparison between anticipatory RD and standard RD as an optimal-control problem.
AI.result: Shows that anticipatory RD achieves at least as high asymptotic average reward as standard RD across all payoff environments, with the minimal achievable cumulative reward gap being zero, establishing global dominance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a "free lunch" exists among no-regret learning dynamics, where anticipatory replicator dynamics uniformly outperforms standard replicator dynamics, meaning a learner can regret not using a particular no-regret algorithm.
